metadata.title: UK Official Development Assistance (ODA)
metadata.description: UK foreign aid spending as reported in the FCDO's Statistics on International Development (SID) publication — the UK's primary official source for ODA data. ODA is measured using the OECD DAC definition and follows cash-flow accounting on a calendar-year basis.

primarySource.name: FCDO — Statistics on International Development
primarySource.notes: Accredited Official Statistics by the Office for Statistics Regulation. Published twice yearly: provisional (spring) and final (autumn). Project-level data included in final release.

crossCheckSource.name: OECD Development Assistance Committee (DAC)
crossCheckSource.notes: OECD provides internationally comparable ODA statistics. UK figures may differ slightly due to rounding and revision cycles.

metadata.methodologyNote: Total ODA includes bilateral aid (government-to-government and project spending) and multilateral contributions (core funding to international organisations). In-donor refugee costs are included in ODA from 2022 onwards per OECD DAC rules, which inflates headline figures. ODA/GNI ratio uses GNI calculated under ESA 2010 methodology from 2016 onwards.

annualODA.description: Total UK ODA by calendar year
annualODA.sourceName: FCDO Statistics on International Development / OECD DAC

series.notes: Peak driven by high levels of debt relief.
First year meeting the UN 0.7% GNI target.
Increase driven by in-donor refugee costs.
Highest bilateral share on record (80.1%). Multilateral fell 47%.

composition2024.sourceName: FCDO Statistics on International Development Final 2024

components.description: Bilateral grants, programmes, and technical cooperation
UK asylum/refugee hosting costs counted as ODA per OECD DAC rules
Core contributions to multilateral organisations (World Bank, UN, etc.)

composition2024.methodologyNote: In-donor refugee costs (£2.827B) are verified from FCDO final statistics. Development aid figure is derived (total minus refugee costs minus multilateral). OECD DAC allows first-year asylum costs to be counted as ODA.

byDepartment2024.sourceName: FCDO Statistics on International Development Final 2024

departments.notes: 67.2% of total. Includes bilateral and multilateral.

departments.fullName: Dept for Energy Security & Net Zero
Dept for Environment, Food & Rural Affairs

byDepartment2024.methodologyNote: Department-level figures from FCDO SID 2024 final publication. FCDO figure includes both bilateral programmes and pass-through multilateral spend.

topRecipients2024.sourceName: FCDO Statistics on International Development Final 2024
topRecipients2024.methodologyNote: Top 3 are confirmed from FCDO final publication. Ranks 4-20 are estimated from regional allocations and FCDO country programme data. Full country-level tables are published in the SID underlying data.

sectorBreakdown2024.sourceName: FCDO Statistics on International Development Final 2024

sectors.notes: Largest single component. OECD DAC-eligible first-year costs.
65% increase from 2023. Includes food, shelter, health emergencies.

sectorBreakdown2024.methodologyNote: Humanitarian aid (£1.453B) is verified from FCDO publication. Refugee costs (£2.827B) verified. Other sector figures are estimated from FCDO thematic summaries. Full sector tables available in SID underlying data.

pctGNITrend.description: ODA as percentage of GNI over time

plannedReduction.notes: Feb 2025: Government announced further reduction to 0.3% of GNI by 2027 to fund defence spending increase.

contextSentences.headline: UK foreign aid fell to 0.50% of GNI in 2024 — down from the 0.70% maintained for seven consecutive years before the 2021 cut.
contextSentences.trend: Total ODA was £14.1bn in 2024, with Ukraine the largest single recipient at £270m.
contextSentences.multilateral: Multilateral contributions fell 47% in 2024 to their lowest level since 2009, as bilateral aid rose to a record 80% share.
contextSentences.future: The Government announced in February 2025 that ODA will be further reduced to 0.3% of GNI by 2027 to fund increased defence spending.
contextSentences.refugees: In-donor refugee costs accounted for £2.83bn (20%) of total ODA in 2024 — the second largest component after FCDO bilateral programmes.
contextSentences.department: FCDO accounted for 67% of all ODA spending. The Home Office was the second-largest spender at £2.38bn, almost entirely on asylum costs.
